name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱخْتَارَ مُوسَىٰ قَوْمَهُۥ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلًا لِّمِيقَـٰتِنَا ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ قَالَ رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُم مِّن قَبْلُ وَإِيَّـٰىَ ۖ أَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ تُضِلُّ بِهَا مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَهْدِى مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ أَنتَ وَلِيُّنَا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَا ۖ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْغَـٰفِرِينَ (١٥٥)
```

```english
Moses chose seventy men from his people for Our tryst, and when the earthquake seized them, he said, ‘My Lord, had You wished, You would have destroyed them and me before. Will You destroy us because of what the fools amongst us have done? It is only Your test, by which You lead astray whomever You wish and guide whomever You wish. You are our master, so forgive us and have mercy on us, for You are the best of those who forgive. (155)
```

```arabic
۞ وَٱكْتُبْ لَنَا فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِنَّا هُدْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ عَذَابِىٓ أُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَنْ أَشَآءُ ۖ وَرَحْمَتِى وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۚ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٥٦)
```

```english
And appoint goodness for us in this world and the Hereafter, for indeed we have come back to You.’ Said He, ‘I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy embraces all things. Soon I shall appoint it for those who are Godwary and give the zakat and those who believe in Our signs (156)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ ٱلْأُمِّىَّ ٱلَّذِى يَجِدُونَهُۥ مَكْتُوبًا عِندَهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَىٰهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَٱلْأَغْلَـٰلَ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلنُّورَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ مَعَهُۥٓ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٥٧)
```

```english
—those who follow the Apostle, the untaught prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them—those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light that has been sent down with him, they are the felicitous.’ (157)
```

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعًا ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ ٱلْأُمِّىِّ ٱلَّذِى يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَٱتَّبِعُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٥٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘O mankind! I am the Apostle of Allah to you all, [of Him] to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. There is no god except Him. He gives life and brings death.’ So have faith in Allah and His Apostle, the untaught prophet, who has faith in Allah and His words, and follow him so that you may be guided. (158)
```

```arabic
وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰٓ أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَبِهِۦ يَعْدِلُونَ (١٥٩)
```

```english
Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide [the people] by the truth and do justice thereby. (159)
```

```arabic
وَقَطَّعْنَـٰهُمُ ٱثْنَتَىْ عَشْرَةَ أَسْبَاطًا أُمَمًا ۚ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱسْتَسْقَىٰهُ قَوْمُهُۥٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْحَجَرَ ۖ فَٱنۢبَجَسَتْ مِنْهُ ٱثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۚ وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْغَمَـٰمَ وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٦٠)
```

```english
We split them up into twelve tribal communities, and We revealed to Moses, when his people asked him for water, [saying], ‘Strike the rock with your staff,’ whereat twelve fountains gushed forth from it. Every tribe came to know its drinking-place. And We shaded them with clouds, and We sent down to them manna and quails: ‘Eat of the good things We have provided you.’ And they did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves. (160)
```

## (Commentary)

These are further chapters from the stories of the Children of Israel, in which Allah mentions many signs (آيات) that He sent down to them and bestowed upon them to guide them to the path of truth and direct them toward the methodology of godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure* — yet they disbelieved in them and wronged themselves.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَاخْتارَ مُوسى قَوْمَهُ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلاً لِمِيقاتِنا **"And Moses chose seventy men from his people for Our appointment"** (7:155). That is, he chose *from* his people; thus, "his people" (قَوْمَهُ) is in the accusative case due to the removal of the preposition.

The verse indicates that Allah, the Glorified, designated an appointment (ميقاتاً) for them, and seventy men, chosen by Moses from the people, attended it. This could only have been for a momentous matter. However, Allah, the Glorified, does not clarify here what the intended purpose of their presence was, other than mentioning that the earthquake (الرجفة) seized them. They were only seized because of a great injustice (ظلم) they committed, which led to their destruction, as evidenced by the saying of Moses (peace be upon him): رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَإِيَّايَ أَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا **"My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them before, and me [as well]. Will You destroy us because of what the fools among us have done?"** (7:155). It appears from this that the earthquake destroyed them.

This is supported by the fact that this story is the one to which the Glorified refers in His saying: وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يا مُوسى لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى اللهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ وَأَنْتُمْ تَنْظُرُونَ ثُمَّ بَعَثْناكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ **"And when you said, ‘O Moses, we will not believe you until we see Allah visibly.’ Thereupon the thunderbolt seized you while you were looking on. Then We raised you up after your death so that you might give thanks"** (2:55-56). And in His saying: يَسْئَلُكَ أَهْلُ الْكِتابِ أَنْ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ كِتاباً مِنَ السَّماءِ فَقَدْ سَأَلُوا مُوسى أَكْبَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ فَقالُوا أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ثُمَّ اتَّخَذُوا الْعِجْلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ فَعَفَوْنا عَنْ ذلِكَ **"The People of the Book ask you to bring down for them a Book from the sky. Certainly they asked Moses for something greater than that, for they said, ‘Show us Allah visibly,’ whereat the thunderbolt seized them for their wrongdoing. Then they took up the Calf [for worship] after the manifest proofs had come to them. Yet We excused that"** (4:153).

From this, it becomes clear that what is meant by the earthquake (الرجفة) that seized them at the appointment was the tremor of the thunderbolt (الصاعقة), not a trembling in their bodies as some commentators have conjectured. There is no harm in this interpretation, as a similar expression appeared previously in the story of the people of Salih, where the Exalted said: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ **"So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless in their homes"** (7:78), while He said regarding them elsewhere: فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صاعِقَةُ الْعَذابِ الْهُونِ **"so the thunderbolt of a humiliating punishment seized them"** (41:17).

In the verse from Surah al-Nisa’ cited above, there is an indication that their request for the vision (الرؤية) was connected to the descent of the Book, and that the taking up of the Calf occurred after that. It is as if they attended the appointment for the descent of the Torah, and they only requested the vision to be certain that it was a heavenly Book revealed from Allah. This is supported by the fact that these chosen men were apparently believers in the essence of Moses's call; they only intended by their saying, "We will not believe you until we see Allah visibly," to make their belief in the descent of the Torah upon him contingent on the vision.

All of this supports the view that this story is part of the narrative of the appointment (الميقات) and the descent of the Torah. When Moses (peace be upon him) intended to attend the appointment of his Lord for the descent of the Torah, he chose these seventy. They went with him to the Mount (الطور) but were not satisfied with Allah speaking to His interlocutor (كليمه); they requested the vision, so the thunderbolt seized them and they died. Then Allah brought them back to life through the prayer of Moses. Subsequently, Allah spoke to Moses, and he requested the vision, and what happened, happened. Among the events was the Children of Israel taking up the Calf after their absence and departure for Allah's appointment. This meaning has occurred in some narrations (الأخبار) transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), as will be mentioned, Allah willing.

In any case, the primary concern in this story is the clarification of their injustice, the descent of punishment upon them, and Moses's prayer for them—not the clarification of whether this story is part of the previous narrative (if it were a part) or distinct from it (if it were distinct). There is no indication in the wording that draws attention to any of that.

As for what has been said—that the apparent state of affairs suggests this story is distinct from the preceding one, because it would not befit eloquence (الفصاحة) to mention part of a story, move to another, and then return to the first, as this would be a disorder from which His speech is protected; and furthermore, that if the earthquake were due to the request for the vision, it would have been said, "Will You destroy us because of what the fools among us *said*," not "what they *did*," and it is not mentioned here that they said anything; nor is it certain that their saying "Show us Allah visibly" occurred here—the truth is that these are three stories: the story of their request for the vision and the descent of the thunderbolt, the story of Moses's appointment and his swooning, and the story of the appointment of the seventy and the seizing by the earthquake. We shall present them in the following "Discussion of Narrations," Allah willing.

For this reason, some have mentioned that this appointment is other than the first appointment. This is because when they worshipped the Calf, Allah commanded Moses to come with a group of them to the Mount to apologize for the worship of the Calf. He chose seventy of them, and they came to the Mount and said what they said, so an earthquake seized their bodies that nearly destroyed them, then it was lifted from them by the prayer of Moses.

Others mentioned that when Aaron died, the Children of Israel accused Moses regarding his matter, saying to him: "You were jealous of him among us, so you killed him." They insisted on this, so he chose seventy of them, among whom was the son of Aaron. They came to his grave, and Moses spoke to him, and Aaron cleared him of his killing. They then said: "We will not be satisfied, O Moses; pray to your Lord to make us prophets." So the earthquake seized them and they swooned.

Others mentioned that the Children of Israel asked Moses for the vision, so he chose the seventy from them. They came to the Mount and said what they said, and the earthquake seized them and they perished, then Allah brought them back to life through the prayer of Moses; however, it is an independent story and not part of the story of Moses.

You are well aware that none of these views—especially the first two—have any evidence from the wording of the Qur'an, nor are they supported by any reliable report (أثر). The partitioning of a single story into multiple narratives and the transition from one discourse to another due to a specific concern is not uncommon in the Noble Qur'an. The Qur'an is not a book of stories such that it would be faulted for transitioning from a story before its completion; rather, it is a book of guidance (هداية), direction (دلالة), and wisdom (حكمة) that takes from stories what is relevant to its purpose.

As for his saying: بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ **"because of what the fools... have done"** (7:155), while what originated from them was a saying (قول) and not an act (فعل), the reason for this is that accountability is only for the sin (المعصية), and a sin is considered a deed (عمل) and an act (فعل) even if it is of the nature of speech. As the Exalted said: إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ **"You are only being requited for what you used to do"** (66:7), which undoubtedly includes the utterance of words of disbelief, lies, slander, and the like. It appears they were punished for the lack of etiquette, stubbornness, and belittling of their Lord's station that their saying necessitated.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of all those views is that they were only punished by the earthquake for what they *said* rather than what they *did*. Thus, the objection, if it were to arise, would apply equally to all views. Therefore, the most likely conclusion is that the story is part of the preceding one, as mentioned before.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَإِيَّايَ **"He said, ‘My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them before, and me [as well]...’"** until His saying: مَنْ تَشاءُ **"whomsoever You wish"** (7:155). He (peace be upon him) intended by this to ask his Lord to bring them back to life, fearing that the Children of Israel would accuse him and thereby abandon the religion, which would nullify his call at its foundation. This is what he sought, but the station and the circumstance prevented him from asking it directly. For here he was, standing before a ruinous sin committed by his people that had struck them down, and a severe divine wrath that had encompassed them until it destroyed them.

Therefore, he began to pave the way slowly, seeking mercy from his Lord with phrases of praise until he could stir mercy over wrath and arouse divine compassion and tenderness. Then he would transition to his request and mention his need in an atmosphere free from the impediments to an answer.

قالَ **"He said,"** beginning with the name of Lordship (الربوبية) which stirs mercy: رَبِّ لَوْ شِئْتَ أَهْلَكْتَهُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ **"My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them before."** The matter is subject to Your will; had You destroyed them before وَإِيَّايَ **"and me [as well],"** no accusation regarding their destruction would have been directed toward me by my people. Then he mentioned that it is not the nature of His mercy nor the sunnah (سنة) — *the established way or law* — of His Lordship to hold a people accountable for the actions of their fools, so he said in the form of a question out of etiquette: أَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا **"Will You destroy us because of what the fools among us have done?"** Then he emphasized the statement by saying: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ **"It is naught but Your trial (فتنتك)"** — *Your test and examination* — تُضِلُّ بِها **"whereby You lead astray"** — i.e., by the trial — مَنْ تَشاءُ وَتَهْدِي مَنْ تَشاءُ **"whomsoever You wish and guide whomsoever You wish."** That is, this instance is one of the instances of Your general trial and testing (ابتلائك) with which You test Your servants and execute upon them, so that he who is led astray is led astray and he who is guided is guided. It is not Your sunnah to destroy everyone who is tested by Your trial and thus deviates from Your straight path (سوي صراطك).

In short: You are the One whose mercy precedes His wrath; it is not Your habit to hasten the punishment of the wrongdoers among Your servants or to punish them for what their fools have done. You are the One who sent me to my people and promised me that You would aid me in the success of my call, and the destruction of these who have swooned brings accusation upon me from my people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** أَنْتَ وَلِيُّنا فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْغافِرِينَ **"You are our guardian (ولينا), so forgive us and have mercy on us, and You are the best of those who forgive"** (7:155). This is his (peace be upon him) commencement of the prayer (الدعاء) after the praise he offered. He began it with His saying: أَنْتَ وَلِيُّنا **"You are our guardian,"** and concluded it with His saying: وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْغافِرِينَ **"and You are the best of those who forgive,"** so that what he asks falls between two attributes: Allah's special guardianship (ولاية) and His forgiveness (مغفرة), which is the best of forgiveness. Then he asked for his need by saying: فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا **"so forgive us and have mercy on us,"** because it is the best need that Allah is pleased for His servants to ask of Him. He did not explicitly state the specific need that prompted the prayer—which was the revival of the seventy whom Allah had destroyed—out of humility and bashfulness.

This need of his is undoubtedly included in His saying: فَاغْفِرْ لَنا وَارْحَمْنا **"so forgive us and have mercy on us."** For Allah mentions in the verse of Surah al-Baqarah that He raised them after their death, and He would not have brought them back to life after destroying them except through the intercession (شفاعة) of Moses (peace be upon him). He did not mention any of his prayer related to their state except this prayer; thus, he only asked for that by way of allusion (تلويحاً) in his saying "so forgive us..." etc., as previously mentioned, not explicitly.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَاكْتُبْ لَنا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ إِنَّا هُدْنا إِلَيْكَ **"And appoint (واكتب) for us good in this world and in the Hereafter. Indeed we have turned (هدنا) to You"** (7:156). That is, we have returned to You; from *hada yahudu* (هاد يهود) when one returns. This—namely His saying: إِنَّا هُدْنا إِلَيْكَ **"Indeed we have turned to You"**—is the justification (تعليل) for this section of the prayer, in which he asked that Allah "write" (يكتب), i.e., decree for them, a good (حسنة) in this world and a good in the Hereafter. What is meant by "good" is undoubtedly a good life and existence. For returning to Allah—meaning following His way and adhering to the path of His primordial nature (الفطرة)—guides man to a pleasant life and a good existence in both this world and the Hereafter. This is the reason for what we mentioned: that His saying "Indeed we have turned to You" is the justification for this section of his prayer, as the pleasant life is among the effects of returning to Allah. It is something they are meant to be provided with—if they are provided—in their future, which is appropriate for "writing" (الكتابة) and decreeing (القضاء). As for the first section of the prayer—namely his saying "so forgive us and have mercy on us" etc.—the sentences preceding it and what surrounded it of His saying "You are our guardian" and "You are the best of those who forgive" are sufficient for its justification, and it does not relate to His saying "Indeed we have turned to You." Understand this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قالَ عَذابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ أَشاءُ وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ **"He said, ‘I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy embraces all things’"** (7:156). This is an answer from the Glorified to Moses, and it corresponds to what Moses offered before his request in his saying: "My Lord! Had You wished, You would have destroyed them before, and me [as well]." Allah, the Glorified, restricted the visitation of His punishment with His saying: مَنْ أَشاءُ **"whomever I wish,"** but did not so restrict the embrace of His mercy. This is because punishment only arises from a requirement on the part of those punished, not from the Glorified Himself. The Exalted said: ما يَفْعَلُ اللهُ بِعَذابِكُمْ إِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ وَآمَنْتُمْ **"What has Allah to do with punishing you if you are grateful and believe?"** (4:147), and He said: لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ **"If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe"** (14:7). Thus, Allah, the Glorified, does not punish by a requirement of His Lordship; were it so, He would punish everyone. Rather, He only punishes some of those whom His will (مشيئته) concerns, and His will only concerns the punishment of those who disbelieve in His blessings. Thus, punishment is only by a requirement on the part of those punished due to their disbelief, not from Him.

Furthermore, His speech, the Glorified, conveys that the reality of punishment is simply the loss of mercy, and vengeance (النقمة) is the non-bestowal of blessing. This only occurs due to the lack of receptivity (استعداد) in the one punished—by means of ingratitude and sin—for the outpouring of blessing upon him and the encompassing of mercy for him. Thus, the cause of punishment is, in reality, the absence of the cause of mercy.

As for the embrace of mercy and the outpouring of blessing, it is well known that this is among the requirements of Divinity (الألوهية) and the necessary concomitants of the attribute of Lordship. There is no created being except that its existence is a blessing for itself and for many below it due to the interconnectedness of the parts of creation. Everything it possesses of good or evil is a blessing, either for itself and others—such as strength, wealth, and other things from which man and others benefit—or for others if it is a misfortune (نقمة) relative to itself, such as defects, pests, and calamities by which one thing is harmed but many things benefit. Accordingly, the divine mercy embraces everything in actuality (فعلاً), not just potentially (شأناً). It is not restricted to a believer or a disbeliever, nor to a sentient or non-sentient being, nor to this world or the Hereafter; and the Will (المشيئة) is necessary to it.

Yes, the occurrence of punishment and misfortune in some instances—which is a relative meaning—necessitates that there be a mercy there that corresponds to it and is measured against it. For the deprivation of some from the blessing that Allah has bestowed upon others, if it is a punishment, then what the others find is a mercy that corresponds to this punishment. Likewise, the descent of what causes pain and harm upon some—such as worldly and otherworldly punishments—if it is a punishment, then the safety and well-being that others find is a mercy relative to it and corresponds to it, even if the absolute mercy (الرحمة المطلقة) in the sense previously explained encompasses them both.

Thus, there is a general divine mercy (رحمة إلهية عامة) by which the believer and the disbeliever, the righteous and the wicked, the sentient and the non-sentient are blessed; they are brought into existence by it and provided for by it at the beginning of their existence and then in the journey of existence as long as they follow the path of survival. And there is a special divine mercy (رحمة إلهية خاصة), which is the wholesome gift that Allah, the Glorified, generously bestows in exchange for faith (الإيمان) and servitude (العبودية). This is necessarily restricted to the righteous believers among His servants, consisting of a pleasant, luminous life in this world, and Paradise and [Divine] Pleasure in the Hereafter; the disbelievers and criminals have no share in it. Corresponding to the special mercy is a punishment, which is the disagreeable state that afflicts the disbelievers and criminals due to their disbelief and crime in this world—such as the punishment of extirpation and a wretched life—and in the Hereafter from the Fire and its pains. Nothing of punishment corresponds to the general mercy, for everything to which the name "thing" (شيء) applies is among the instances of general mercy for itself or for others; its being a mercy is what is intended in creation, and there is nothing beyond "thing."

When this is established, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: عَذابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ أَشاءُ وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ **"I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy embraces all things"** is a clarification of the specificity of punishment and the universality of mercy. He only contrasted punishment with general mercy—despite their lack of direct opposition—because the mention of general mercy is a preamble and preparation for what He will mention of its becoming a special mercy for the God-conscious among the believers.

It has become clear from the foregoing that the embrace of mercy is not merely potential, and that His saying "but My mercy embraces all things" is not restricted by a conditional will; rather, it is among the necessary concomitants of actual (فعلية) encompassing mercy, as mentioned. This is because the apparent meaning of the verse is that what is meant by mercy is the general mercy, which embraces everything in actuality, and Allah has willed that, so it is necessary to it. Thus, there is no place for an implied "if I wish," contrary to the apparent words of a group of commentators.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَسَأَكْتُبُها لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ **"I will appoint (فسأكتبها) it for those who are God-conscious and give the zakat, and those who believe in Our signs"** (7:156). This is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: "I visit My punishment on whomever I wish, but My mercy..." the verse. That is, the necessity of the inevitability of punishment visiting some people and the embrace of mercy for everything is that I make mercy mandatory for the remaining group, and they are those who are God-conscious and give the zakat... the verse.

The Glorified has mentioned those who attain mercy with general descriptions, which are godwariness (التقوى), the giving of zakat (إيتاء الزكاة), and faith in the signs of Allah, without restricting them to what is specific to his people—such as saying "for those who are God-conscious among you" or the like—because that is what the universality of the clarification in His saying "I visit My punishment on whomever I wish..." necessitates. A general clarification yields a general result.

When Moses's request is compared with the verse, the verse stands as a restriction (مقيدة) for it. For he (peace be upon him) asked for the good (الحسنة) and mercy for his people, then justified it by his saying: "Indeed we have turned to You." The meaning of that was a request for mercy for everyone who turned and returned from among them, such that Allah would decree for them the good of this world and the Hereafter merely for their turning and returning to Him. Thus, in what Allah answered him with, He stated that He would decree His mercy for those who believe and are God-conscious. It is as if he said: "Appoint Your mercy for whoever has turned to You from us," and Allah answered him: "I will appoint My mercy for whoever has turned, been God-conscious, and believed in My signs." Thus, there was a restriction of his request.

There is no harm in this, for He, the Glorified, is the Guide for His prophets and messengers, their Teacher. He teaches His interlocutor (كليمه) to restrict his request to godwariness—which is refraining from His prohibitions—and to faith in His signs—which is submission to His prophets and the laws revealed to them—and not to leave "turning" (الهود), which is returning to Allah by believing in Him, absolute. This is an intervention in Moses's prayer by restricting it, just as He, the Exalted, intervened in Abraham's prayer with restriction in His saying: قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً قالَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي قالَ لا يَنالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ **"He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’ Said he, ‘And from among my descendants?’ He said, ‘My pledge does not extend to the unjust’"** (2:124), and with generalization and absolute application in His saying regarding what He narrates of his prayer for the people of Mecca: وَارْزُقْ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ قالَ وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُ قَلِيلاً ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُ إِلى عَذابِ النَّارِ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ **"and provide its people with fruits—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day.’ He said, ‘As for him who is faithless, I will let him enjoy for a little while, then I will compel him to the punishment of the Fire, and it is an evil destination’"** (2:126).

It has thus become clear, firstly, that the verse includes His, the Exalted, acceptance of Moses's prayer: "And appoint for us good in this world and in the Hereafter," by restricting its scope. It is therefore strange what some have mentioned: that the verse in its context indicates that Allah, the Glorified, rejected Moses's call and did not accept it. Likewise, the saying of some: that Moses (peace be upon him) prayed for his people, and Allah accepted it in favor of the nation of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny), based on the explanatory nature of His saying: "Those who follow the Apostle..." the verse, for His saying: "for those who are God-conscious..." the verse, as will come.

Secondly: He, the Exalted, accepted what the first section of his prayer contained, for He, the Exalted, did not reject it. It is far-fetched that Allah would narrate in His speech a futile prayer that was not accepted. His saying: "I will appoint it for those..." the verse, corresponds to what he (peace be upon him) asked of the continuous, lasting good in this world and the Hereafter for his people. As for the request for forgiveness for a momentary sin that originated from them in their saying "Show us Allah visibly," His saying "I will appoint it..." the verse, does not correspond to it in any way. Thus, His, the Exalted, silence regarding the rejection of his call is evidence of its acceptance, as in other similar instances in the Qur'an.

An allusion to the acceptance of his prayer for their forgiveness is found in His saying in the story in another place: ثُمَّ بَعَثْناكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ **"Then We raised you up after your death so that you might give thanks"** (2:56). It is highly improbable that Allah would bring them back to life after destroying them without having forgiven them the sin for which they were destroyed.

In any case, the meaning of the verse: فَسَأَكْتُبُها **"I will appoint it"**—that is, I will write My mercy, decree it, and make it mandatory. "Writing" (الكتابة) is used metaphorically for making mandatory because writing is more firm and established. لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ **"for those who are God-conscious"** and avoid sins and the neglect of obligations. وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ **"and give the zakat,"** which is the financial right or absolute spending in the way of Allah by which wealth grows, the corruptions of society are rectified, and its deficiencies are completed. It has been said that what is meant by it is the purification (زكاة) of the soul and its cleanliness, and "giving the zakat" is the rectification of the soul's character; but this is of no consequence.

وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ **"and those who believe in Our signs"**—that is, they submit to the signs and marks that have come to them from Allah, whether they be miraculous signs (آيات معجزة) like the miracles of Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (peace be upon him and them), or heavenly laws like the legislations of Moses and his commands and the legislations of other prophets, or the prophets themselves, or the marks of the prophets' truthfulness like the marks of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) which Allah the Exalted mentioned to them in the Book of Moses and Jesus (peace be upon them). All of these are signs for Him, the Exalted, in which they and others must believe and to which they must submit, and which they must not deny.

In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the context of speaking with another (the first person plural) to the third person (the absent). For He said first: "And Moses chose seventy men from his people for Our appointment (لِمِيقاتِنا)." Then He said: "He said, ‘I visit My punishment (عَذابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ)...’" the verse. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is the manifestation of His, the Glorified, special concern for accepting the prayers of the supplicants among His servants; thus, He, the Exalted, turns toward them without anyone else sharing in it, even by mediation. For speaking with the term of the speaker with another is to manifest greatness, because the great speak on behalf of themselves and their followers. When a special concern for the addressee or the discourse is intended, one speaks with the term of the speaker alone.

Upon this, His speech, the Exalted, proceeded; thus, He chose the context of the speaker alone, which is appropriate for the meaning of intimate conversation (المناجاة) and secret discourse (المسارة) in what He narrated of the prayers of His prophets and friends and His acceptance of them in His speech—such as the prayers of Noah and Abraham, the prayer of Moses on the night of the Mount, and the prayers of other righteous ones and His acceptance of them. He did not deviate from the context of the speaker alone except for an additional subtle point.

As for His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ **"and those who believe in Our signs,"** and the deviation therein from the speaker alone—the previous context—to the speaker with another, it appears that the subtle point in it is to create a connection between this verse and the following verse, which is a kind of clarification for this phrase, namely His saying: "and those who believe in Our signs." For the following verse—as will come—is in the position of a parenthetical statement (المعترضة) from the result taken within the ongoing discourse. Its context is a context outside the context of this segment concerned with the direct address and intimate conversation between Moses and Him, the Exalted, returning to the original previous context, which is the context of the speaker with another.

By changing "and those who believe in My signs" to His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِآياتِنا يُؤْمِنُونَ **"and those who believe in Our signs,"** the following verse connects to its predecessor in the context in a subtle manner. Understand this and reflect upon it, for it is among the most wondrous of Qur'anic contexts.

**His saying, the Exalted:** الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ **"those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, whom they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel"** until His saying: كانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ **"that were upon them"** (7:157). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The shackle (الإصر) is the tying of a thing and its imprisonment by force... The Exalted said: 'and relieves them of their shackle (إِصْرَهُمْ),' meaning the matters that hinder them and bind them from good deeds and from reaching rewards. Likewise: 'and do not lay upon us a shackle (إِصْراً).' It is said: a burden (ثقلاً), and its realization is what I mentioned." (End quote). And "the fetters" (الأغلال) is the plural of *ghull* (غل), which is that with which one is bound.

His saying: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ **"those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet"** the verse, according to the apparent context, is a clarification (بيان) for His saying: "and those who believe in Our signs." This is supported by what is apparent in the verse: that his (peace be upon him and his progeny) being an apostle, an uninstructed prophet, who bids them to what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful for them the good things and forbids them the corrupt things, and relieves them of their shackle and the fetters that were upon them—all of this is among the signs of the final prophethood and its marks mentioned for them in the Torah and the Evangel. Thus, part of the faith in the signs of Allah, which Allah the Exalted stipulated for them in His speech, is that they believe in the signs mentioned for them as marks for the prophethood of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny).

However, it is an established matter about which there is no doubt that the mercy which Allah promised to decree for the Jews on the condition of godwariness and faith in the signs of Allah is not such that it is restricted to those of them who believed in the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), while the righteous of the Children of Israel—from the time Allah accepted Moses's call until Allah sent Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) and a small group of Jews believed in him—are deprived of it. This is something that should not be imagined at all. For between Moses and Jesus (peace be upon them), and likewise after Jesus (peace be upon him), among those of the Children of Israel who believed in him, there was a vast multitude of believers who believed in the divine call; Allah accepted their faith from them and promised them good, and the divine speech is vocal with that. How then can the divine mercy spread over the Children of Israel be restricted to a small group of them who believed in the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny)?

Thus, His saying: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ **"those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet"** the verse, even if it is a clarification for His saying: "and those who believe in Our signs," is not a clarification equal in breadth and narrowness to its subject; rather, it is a clarification extracted from its subject, singled out for mention to be utilized in what is the purpose of the discourse's flow—namely, the clarification of the reality of the Muhammadan call and the necessity of their answering it and responding to its caller.

For this reason, there are parallels in the Noble Qur'an in terms of narrowing and broadening in clarification, as the Exalted said, narrating from Iblis: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ **"By Your might, I will surely pervert them all"** (38:82), then He said in another place narrating from him: لَأَتَّخِذَنَّ مِنْ عِبادِكَ نَصِيباً مَفْرُوضاً وَلَأُضِلَّنَّهُمْ وَلَأُمَنِّيَنَّهُمْ وَلَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُبَتِّكُنَّ آذانَ الْأَنْعامِ وَلَآمُرَنَّهُمْ فَلَيُغَيِّرُنَّ خَلْقَ اللهِ **"I will surely take of Your servants a settled share, and I will surely lead them astray and give them [false] hopes, and I will surely prompt them to slit the ears of cattle, and I will surely prompt them to alter Allah’s creation"** (4:118-119). The second statement narrated from Iblis is extracted from the generality of his statement narrated first: "I will surely pervert them all."

And the Exalted said at the beginning of this Surah: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْناكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْناكُمْ **"Certainly We created you, then We formed you"** until He said: يا بَنِي آدَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ **"O Children of Adam! If there come to you apostles from among yourselves"** (7:11, 35). It has already been mentioned that this is of the nature of extracting a discourse from a discourse for the purpose of generalizing to others, among other parallels.

Thus, the meaning of the explanatory nature of His saying: "those who follow the Apostle" boils down to extracting a clarification from a clarification for application to the case of need. It is as if it were said: "If the decree of Allah's mercy for the Children of Israel has been written for those who are God-conscious, give the zakat, and believe in Our signs, then its manifestation today—the day of Muhammad's (peace be upon him and his progeny) mission—is those of the Children of Israel who follow him. For they are the ones who have been God-conscious and given the zakat, and they are the ones who have believed in Our signs; for they believed in Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (peace be upon him and them), and they are Our signs; and they believed in the miracles of these messengers and the laws and rulings revealed to them, and they are Our signs; and they believed in what We mentioned to them in the Torah and the Evangel of the marks of Muhammad's (peace be upon him and his progeny) prophethood and the signs of his appearance and call, and they are Our signs."

Furthermore, in His saying: الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ **"those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet"** the verse, "follow" (يَتَّبِعُونَ) is taken in place of "believe," and it is among the most beautiful of expressions. This is because faith in the signs of Allah, the Glorified—such as His prophets and their laws—is only through submission and obedience. Thus, the word "following" (الاتباع) was chosen to indicate that faith in the sense of mere abstract belief is of no avail; for the abandonment of submission and obedience in practice is a denial of the signs of Allah, even if there is a belief that they are true.

His mentioning him (peace be upon him and his progeny) with these three attributes: الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ **"the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet,"** which are not gathered for him in any place in His speech except in this verse and the following verse, along with His saying afterward: الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ **"whom they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel,"** indicates that he (peace be upon him and his progeny) was mentioned in them and identified by these three attributes.

Were it not that the purpose of describing him with these three was to identify him by what they knew of him from the traits mentioned for him in their two Books, there would be no apparent subtle point for the mention of the three: "the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet," especially the third attribute.

Likewise, the apparent meaning of the verse indicates or suggests that His saying: يَأْمُرُهُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ **"bids them to what is right and forbids them from what is wrong"** until the end of the five matters with which He described him (peace be upon him and his progeny) in the verse, are among his marks mentioned in the two Books. They are, moreover, among the specifics of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) and his luminous religion. For although the righteous nations used to perform the duty of bidding to what is right and forbidding what is wrong—as He, the Exalted, mentioned regarding the People of the Book in His saying: لَيْسُوا سَواءً مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ أُمَّةٌ قائِمَةٌ **"They are not all alike. Of the People of the Book is an upright community"** until He said: وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَيُسارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْراتِ وَأُولئِكَ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ **"bidding what is right and forbidding what is wrong, and racing with one another in good works, and they are among the righteous"** (3:113-114).

Likewise, the making lawful of good things and the forbidding of corrupt things in general is among the primordial matters (الفطريات) upon which the divine religions have agreed, and He, the Exalted, has said: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ **"Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’"** (7:32).

Likewise, the relieving of the shackle and the fetters, even if it is found to some extent in the law of Jesus (peace be upon him)—as indicated by His saying regarding what Allah narrated from him in the Noble Qur'an: وَمُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ **"and [I come] to confirm what is before me of the Torah, and to make lawful to you some of what was forbidden to you"** (3:50), and suggested by his saying addressing the Children of Israel: قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ **"I have certainly brought you wisdom, and to clarify for you some of what you differ about"** (43:63).

However, no doubter can doubt that the religion brought by Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) with a Book from Allah confirming what was before it of the heavenly Books—which is the religion of Islam—is the only religion that breathed into the body of bidding to what is right and forbidding what is wrong all the spirit of life it could contain, and brought it from the level of mere call to the degree of struggle (الجهاد) in the way of Allah with wealth and lives. It is the only religion that enumerated all the affairs and actions related to human life and then divided them into good things (طيبات), which it made lawful, and corrupt things (خبائث), which it forbade. No religious law or social law equals it in the detail of the legislated laws. It is the religion that abrogated all the difficult rulings placed upon the People of the Book and the Jews specifically, and what their scholars burdened themselves with, and what their rabbis and monks innovated of the invented rulings.

Thus, Islam is distinguished by the perfection of these five matters, even if models of them are found in others.

Furthermore, the perfection of these five matters in this luminous religion is the truest witness and clearest proof (بينة) of the truthfulness of the one who rose with its call (peace be upon him and his progeny), even if marks for him were not mentioned in the two Books. For his law is the perfection of the law of the Interlocutor (Moses) and the Messiah (peace be upon them). Is anything sought from a true law except its recognition of what is right (المعروف), its rejection of what is wrong (المنكر), its making lawful of good things, its forbidding of corrupt things, and its annulment of every shackle and fetter? These are the details of the truth to which the divine laws call. So let the people of the Torah and the Evangel recognize that the law which includes the perfection of these matters in their details is the very essence of their law in a complete stage.

By this clarification, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: يَأْمُرُهُمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ **"bids them to what is right and forbids them from what is wrong"** the verse, conveys in its totality the meaning of his confirmation of what is in their two Books of Allah's laws. It is as if it were said: "confirming what is before him," as in His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ نَبَذَ فَرِيقٌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ كِتابَ اللهِ وَراءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ **"And when there came to them an apostle from Allah, confirming what was with them, a part of those who were given the Book cast the Book of Allah behind their backs, as if they did not know"** (2:101), and His saying: وَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ كِتابٌ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَهُمْ وَكانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ ما عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ فَلَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ **"And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming what was with them—and earlier they would pray for victory over the faithless—so when there came to them what they recognized, they defied it. So may the curse of Allah be on the faithless"** (2:89). He intends the coming of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) with the perfection of what is in their Book of the law, confirming it, then their disbelief in him while they know that he is the one mentioned in their Books, the one given glad tidings of by the tongues of their prophets, as the Glorified narrated from the Messiah in His saying: يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ وَمُبَشِّراً بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِي اسْمُهُ أَحْمَدُ **"O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah, and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad"** (61:6).

We shall discuss his (peace be upon him and his progeny) glad tidings occurring in their sacred books with what is possible of research, Allah the Mighty willing.

However, He, the Exalted, did not say "confirming what is before him" in place of His saying "bids them to what is right..." the verse, because the address is directed to all people, not to the People of the Book specifically. Therefore, He commanded His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) in the following verse by saying: قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً **"Say, ‘O mankind! I am the apostle of Allah to you all’"** (7:158), and He did not restrict the speech in His saying: فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ **"So those who believe in him"** etc., to what is specific to the People of the Book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ **"So those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light"** until the end of the verse (7:157). "Honouring" (التعزير) is aid accompanied by veneration. What is meant by "the light" (النور) revealed with him is the Noble Qur'an. It is mentioned with the attribute of luminosity to indicate that it illuminates the path of life and lights the way that man traverses to the station of happiness and perfection; and the discourse is in this regard.

In His saying, the Exalted: أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ **"revealed with him,"** and He did not say "revealed to him" or "revealed toward him," and "with" (مع) indicates companionship and accompaniment, there is an allusion to the meaning of the mark and witness that we mentioned. It is as if it were said: "and followed the light that was revealed to him," and it—by what it contains of the perfection of the previous laws and what it manifests by illumination—is a witness to his truthfulness and a mark that he is the one promised by their prophets and mentioned to them in their Books. Thus, His saying "with him" (مَعَهُ) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pro-agent of "revealed" (أُنْزِلَ). Its parallel occurred in His saying, the Exalted: فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ **"so Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed"** (2:213).

The commentators have differed in justifying this accompaniment and its meaning. It has been said that the adverbial phrase "with him" (مَعَهُ) is related to "revealed," and the speech is based on the omission of a possessed noun, i.e., "with his prophethood" or "his mission" (peace be upon him and his progeny), because it was not revealed *with* him, but rather revealed with Gabriel. It has been said it is related to "followed" (اتَّبَعُوا), and the meaning is that they shared with the Prophet (peace be upon him) in his following [of the light], or the meaning is they followed the Qur'an along with their following of him. It has been said it is a circumstantial qualifier for the agent of "followed," and the meaning is they followed the Qur'an while accompanying the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) in his following. It has been said that "with" (مع) here is in the sense of "upon" (على), and it has been said it is in the sense of "at" (عند). The remoteness of all these views is not hidden.

His saying: فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ **"So those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light"** the verse, is in the position of an explanation for His saying at the beginning of the verse: "those who follow the Apostle," and that what is meant by following him is, in reality, following the Book of Allah which contains his laws, and that what he (peace be upon him and his progeny) has of the meaning of following is faith in his prophethood and mission without denying him, and respecting him by submitting to him and aiding him in what he has resolved upon of his conduct.

The speech—namely His saying: "So those who believe in him" the verse—is a result derived from His saying at the beginning of the verse: "those who follow the Apostle" the verse, based on what we presented: that it is a specific clarification extracted from His saying: "and those who believe in Our signs," which is a general clarification. The meaning is: if following the Apostle with these attributes and traits is part of the faith in Our signs which We stipulated for the Children of Israel in accepting Moses's call for them by spreading mercy in this world and the Hereafter—and in it is success (الفلاح) by the decreeing of good in this world and the Hereafter—then those who believe in him—until the end of what Allah stipulated—they are the successful.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً **"Say, ‘O mankind! I am the apostle of Allah to you all’"** until His saying: وَيُمِيتُ **"and gives death"** (7:158). Since it appeared from the attributes with which He described His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) that he possesses the perfection of the religion by which the pleasant life of people is attained in any place assumed and in any time existence is estimated—and people have no need for the pleasantness of their life for more than that they be bidden to what is right, forbidden from what is wrong, that good things be made lawful for them, corrupt things be forbidden to them, and their shackle and the fetters upon them be relieved—He commanded His Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) to announce his prophethood to all people without it being restricted to one people over another, so He said: "Say, ‘O mankind! I am the apostle of Allah to you all’."

His saying: الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحيِي وَيُمِيتُ **"to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. There is no god except Him. He gives life and gives death"** are attributes with which He described Allah. In their totality, they are in the position of a justification (تعليل) by which He clarifies the possibility of the mission from Allah in itself first, and the possibility of its universality to all people second. By this, the aversion of the Children of Israel to an apostle being sent to them from outside their tribe is removed—especially from the uninstructed (الأميين), while they are the people of Allah and among their claims is that they have no obligation toward the uninstructed, and they are the elite of Allah, His children, and His beloveds. By this also, the sense of improbability among non-Arabs due to nationalistic fervor that an Arab apostle be sent to them is removed.

That is because Allah, who took him as an apostle, is the One to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and the general authority over them. There is no god other than Him such that he would own anything of them. Thus, He has the right to judge as He wishes without any preventer hindering His judgment, competing with Him, or any will obstructing His will. Thus, He has the right to take an apostle to His servants and to send His apostle to some of His servants or to all of them as He wishes.

He is the One to whom belongs the giving of life and the giving of death. Thus, He has the right to give life to a people or to all people with a pleasant, happy life. Happiness and guidance (الهدى) are part of life, just as wretchedness and misguidance are death. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ **"O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life"** (8:24), and He said: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ **"Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people..."** (6:122), and He said: إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ ، وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ **"Only those who listen will respond; as for the dead, Allah will resurrect them"** (6:36).

**His saying, the Exalted:** فَآمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ النَّبِيِّ الْأُمِّيِّ **"So have faith in Allah and His Apostle, the uninstructed prophet"** until the end of the verse (7:158). This is a derivation from what preceded. That is: if the state of affairs is this, then believe in me, for I am that Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, who was given glad tidings of in the Torah and the Evangel. I believe in Allah and do not disbelieve in Him, and I believe in His words (كلماته)—which are what He has decreed of the revealed laws upon me and upon the previous prophets—and follow me so that you may be successful.

This is what the context necessitates. From it, the reason for the transition from the first person to the third person in His saying "and His Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, who..." the verse, is known. For the apparent context is that this verse is the conclusion of the previous verse, and they are both from the speech of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny).

The reason for the transition—as appeared from the foregoing—is to indicate, by the attributes placed in the position of the speaker's pronoun, the justification for the command in His saying "So have faith" and His saying: وَاتَّبِعُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ **"and follow him so that you may be guided."**

What is meant by being guided (الاهتداء) is being guided to the happiness of the Hereafter, which is Allah's pleasure and Paradise, not being guided to the path of truth. For faith in Allah and His Apostle and following His Apostle is itself guidance. Thus, the meaning of His saying "so that you may be guided" returns to the meaning of His saying in the previous verse regarding the result of faith and following: "they are the successful."

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَمِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ **"Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby"** (7:159). This is part of the fairness of the Qur'an: praising those who deserve praise and commending their righteous deeds after having rebuked them for the evil deeds that originated from them. The meaning is that they are not all as We described of opposing Allah and His Apostle and adhering to misguidance and injustice; rather, among them is a community (أمة) who guide people by the truth and by the truth they do justice among themselves. The "by" (الباء) in His saying بِالْحَقِّ **"by the truth"** is for the instrument (للآلة) and potentially for accompaniment (الملابسة).

Accordingly, the verse is among the instances in which guidance (الهداية) is attributed to other than Him, the Exalted, and other than the prophets and Imams, as in His saying narrating from the believer of the family of Pharaoh—who was apparently not a prophet: وَقالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ **"He who had faith said, ‘O my people! Follow me, I will guide you to the way of rectitude’"** (40:38).

It is not improbable that what is meant by this community from the people of Moses (peace be upon him) are the prophets and Imams who arose among them after Moses. Allah has described them in His speech with guidance, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا وَكانُوا بِآياتِنا يُوقِنُونَ **"And We appointed among them imams to guide by Our command when they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs"** (32:24) and other verses. This is because the verse—namely His saying "a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby"—if taken in its true sense of guiding by the truth and doing justice by the truth, would not be possible for anyone other than a prophet or an Imam to be characterized by it. A discussion on guidance has already been presented in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind’" (2:124) and His saying: "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He expands his breast..." (6:125) and other verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَقَطَّعْناهُمُ اثْنَتَيْ عَشْرَةَ أَسْباطاً أُمَماً **"We divided them into twelve tribes (أَسْباطاً), [as distinct] nations"** until the end of the verse (7:160). The "tribe" (السبط) according to the language is the child of the child or the child of the daughter. The plural is *asbat* (أسباط). Among the Children of Israel, it means a specific people; thus, the "tribe" for them is in the position of the "clan" (القبيلة) for the Arabs. It has been transmitted from Ibn al-Hajib that "tribes" (أَسْباطاً) in the verse is a substitute (بدل) for the number, not a specification (تمييز); otherwise, they would have been thirty-six tribes, assuming the minimum plural for "tribes." The specification of the number is omitted because it is indicated by His saying "tribes," and the estimation is: "We divided them into twelve groups, tribes." It has also been said that it is a specification because it is in the sense of the singular, and the meaning is "twelve groups," for example.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَى مُوسَى إِذِ اسْتَسْقَاهُ قَوْمُهُ** "And We revealed to Moses, when his people asked him for water..." (7:160). The term "gushing forth" (الانبجاس) means bursting out. It is also said that *al-inbijas* refers to the water coming out in a small amount, while "erupting" (الانفجار) refers to it coming out in abundance. It is evident from His saying: **فَانْبَجَسَتْ مِنْهُ اثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ** "...whereat there gushed forth from it twelve fountains. Every tribe came to know its drinking-place" (7:160), that the springs were equal to the number of the tribes (الأسباط), and that each tribe was designated a specific spring. This suggests that there had been disputes and competition among them, which supports what is mentioned in the narrations regarding this story. The remainder of the verse is clear.

In these verses, Allah, the Glorified, has enumerated several of the miracles (المعجزات) and signs (الآيات) of Moses (عليه السلام): the serpent, the white hand, the years of famine for the people of Pharaoh and the diminution of their fruits, the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, the blood, the parting of the sea, the destruction of the seventy men and their subsequent revival, the gushing of springs from the rock by the striking of the staff, the shading by the clouds, the sending down of manna and quails, and the raising of the mountain above them as if it were a canopy. To these, you may add Allah’s speaking to him, the revelation of the Torah, and the transformation of some of them into "despised apes." A detailed discussion of his (عليه السلام) story will follow in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Hud, if Allah wills.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Muhammad ibn Salim, the reed-seller, from al-Harith ibn al-Mughirah, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: I said to him: "Abdullah ibn Ajlan said during the illness in which he died that he would not die, yet he died." He (the Imam) said: "May Allah not forgive any of his sins! Where did he go? Indeed, Moses chose seventy men from his people, and when the earthquake seized them, he said: 'O Lord, my companions! My companions!' Allah said: 'I shall replace them for you with those who are better for you than them.' Moses said: 'I knew them and I perceived their scent.' So Allah sent prophets to him."

**I say:** The meaning is that Allah replaced Abdullah ibn Ajlan for the Imam with companions who were better than him, just as He did for Moses. This report is unique in its genre and does not align with the apparent meaning of the Book.

In *al-Burhan*, from Ibn Babawayh, by his chain of transmission from Sa'd ibn Abdullah al-Qummi, in a long tradition from al-Qa'im (عليه السلام), who said: I said: "Inform me, O my master, of the reason that prevents the people from choosing an Imam for themselves." He said: "A righteous one (مصلح) or a corrupt one (مفسد)?" I said: "A righteous one." He said: "Is it possible that their choice might fall upon a corrupt one, since none of them knows what occurs in the mind of another regarding righteousness or corruption?" I said: "Yes." He said: "That is the reason for which I shall provide you a proof:

Inform me about the Messengers whom Allah chose, revealed the Book to, and supported with infallibility (العصمة)—since they are the most knowledgeable of nations (أعلم الأمم) and the best guided to make a choice—such as Moses and Jesus. Is it possible, despite the abundance of their intellect and the perfection of their knowledge, that when they intend to make a choice, their choice falls upon a hypocrite (المنافق) while they think he is a believer?" I said: "No." He said: "This is Moses, the one to whom Allah spoke (كليم الله), despite the abundance of his intellect, the perfection of his knowledge, and the descent of revelation upon him; he chose from the elite of his people and the leaders of his army for the appointed time of his Lord seventy men whose faith and sincerity he did not doubt. Yet, his choice fell upon hypocrites. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَاخْتَارَ مُوسَى قَوْمَهُ سَبْعِينَ رَجُلًا لِّمِيقَاتِنَا** 'And Moses chose seventy men from his people for Our tryst...' (7:155) until His saying: **لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى نَرَى اللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ بِظُلْمِهِمْ** '...We will not believe you until we see Allah visibly. Thereupon the thunderbolt seized them for their wrongdoing' (4:153)."

When we find that the choice of one whom Allah has chosen for prophethood fell upon the most corrupt rather than the most righteous—while he thought him to be the most righteous—we know that the choice belongs only to Him who knows what the chests hide, what the consciences conceal, and to whom the secrets are turned. Thus, the choice of the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار) is of no consequence, given that the choice of the prophets fell upon the corrupt when they desired the righteous.

**I say:** The verse therein is quoted by its meaning, in the sense that it is a composite of verses regarding the story found in Surahs al-A'raf and al-Nisa'.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Nawf al-Himyari, who said: "When Moses chose seventy men from his people for the appointed time of his Lord, Allah said to Moses: 'I shall make the earth a place of prostration (مسجداً) and a means of purification (طهوراً) for you; I shall place tranquility (السكينة) with you in your houses; and I shall make you recite the Torah from memory—so that the man among you, the woman, the free, the slave, the young, and the old shall recite it.'

Moses said: 'Allah has made the earth a place of prostration and a means of purification for you.' They said: 'We do not wish to pray except in churches.' He said: 'And He shall place tranquility with you in your houses.' They said: 'We do not want it except as it was in the Ark (التابوت).' He said: 'And He shall make you recite the Torah from memory—so that the man among you, the woman, the free, the slave, the young, and the old shall recite it.' They said: 'We do not wish to recite it except by looking at it.' Allah said: **فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكَاةَ** '...so I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious and give the alms...' (7:156) until His saying: **الْمُفْلِحُونَ** '...the successful' (7:157).

Moses said: 'I came to You with a delegation of my people, yet You have granted their delegation to others; make me of this nation (الأمة).' He said: 'Their prophet is from among them.' He said: 'Make me of this nation.' He said: 'You shall not reach them.' He said: 'O Lord, I came to You with a delegation of my people, yet You have granted their delegation to others.' So He revealed to him: **وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** 'Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:159). Nawf said: 'Do you not praise a Lord who witnessed your absence, took up your cause, and granted the delegation of others to you?'"

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Nawf al-Bakali: "When Moses chose seventy men from his people, he said to them: 'Go as a delegation to Allah and ask Him.' Moses had a request and they had a request. When they reached the Mount (الطور), the place Allah had promised him, Moses said to them: 'Ask Allah.' They said: **أَرِنَا اللَّهَ جَهْرَةً** '...Show us Allah visibly' (4:153). He said: 'Woe to you! Do you ask Allah for this twice?' They said: 'This is our request: Show us Allah visibly.' So the earthquake seized them and they fell unconscious. Moses said: 'O Lord, I brought to You seventy of the best of the Children of Israel; should I return to them while none of them is with me? What shall I do with the Children of Israel? Will they not kill me?' He said to him: 'Ask your request.' He said: 'O Lord, I ask You to resurrect them.' So Allah resurrected them. Thus, their request and his request were gone, and that supplication was granted to this nation."

**I say:** We have cited these two narrations because, in their account of the story, they resemble "stopped" reports (الموقوفات) — *narrations that reach the companions but not the Prophet*. However, despite their differences, they do not align with any part of the story or the revelation of the verses as they appear. Their request was for the vision (الرؤية), and it was rejected. Moses' (عليه السلام) request was for their resurrection, and it was answered, so they were resurrected. The decreeing of mercy for the Children of Israel was answered on the condition of godwariness (التقوى) and faith in the signs of Allah. None of their delegation was granted to others. Furthermore, the address in His saying: **وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** "Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby" (7:159) is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) rather than Moses, as indicated by the context.

Similar to these two narrations in their lack of alignment with the verse is what was narrated from Ibn Abbas: "Regarding His saying: **وَاكْتُبْ لَنَا فِي هَذِهِ الدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** 'And appoint for us good in this world and the Hereafter...' (7:156), he said: 'It was not given to Moses.' He said: **عَذَابِي أُصِيبُ بِهِ مَنْ أَشَاءُ** '...My punishment—I visit it on whomever I wish...' (7:156) until His saying: **الْمُفْلِحُونَ** '...the successful' (7:157). The meaning is that it was not given to him, but rather given to this nation." It has already been mentioned that the apparent meaning of the verse indicates otherwise.

A similar case is what was narrated from al-Suddi: "Regarding His saying: **إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ** '...It is naught but Your trial...' (7:155), he said: 'Moses said: O Lord, this Samiri commanded them to take the calf; did You see the spirit—who breathed it into it? The Lord said: I did. He said: Then it was You who led them astray.'" Al-Ayyashi narrated something similar in his *Tafsir* from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) as a disconnected report (مرسلاً), in which it says: "Moses said: 'O Lord, who made the calf low?' He said: 'I did.' Moses then said: 'It is naught but Your trial (فتنتك)—whereby You lead astray whomever You wish and guide whomever You wish.'"

This is because the verse—that is, His saying: **إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا فِتْنَتُكَ**—is from his (عليه السلام) speech during the story of the destruction of the seventy. What does it have to do with the story of the calf? Unless he repeated that statement.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and Abu Dawud narrated from Jundub ibn Abdullah al-Bajali, who said: "A Bedouin came, made his camel kneel, hobbled it, and then prayed behind the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Then he called out: 'O Allah, have mercy on me and Muhammad, and do not include anyone else in our mercy.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'You have restricted a vast mercy. Indeed, Allah created a hundred mercies; He sent down one mercy by which the creation—its jinn, humans, and beasts—show compassion to one another, and He has ninety-nine with Him.'"

In the same source, Ahmad and Muslim narrated from Salman from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Indeed, Allah has a hundred mercies. From them is one mercy by which the creation shows mercy to one another, and by which the wild beasts show affection to their offspring. The other ninety-nine are for the Day of Resurrection."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Salman as a "stopped" report (موقوفاً), and Ibn Mardawayh from Salman, who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed, Allah created a hundred mercies on the day He created the heavens and the earth. Each mercy is as vast as the space between the heaven and the earth. He sent down one mercy to the earth, by which the creatures show mercy to one another, by which the mother shows affection to her child, by which the birds and wild beasts drink water, and by which the creatures live. When the Day of Resurrection comes, He shall withdraw it from His creation and then pour it upon the God-conscious (المتقين), adding ninety-nine mercies.' Then he recited: **وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ** '...My mercy embraces all things. Soon I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious...' (7:156)."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated through Shi'i chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). The second narration seems to be a transmission of the meaning of the first, but the narrator corrupted the sense by saying: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, He shall withdraw it from His creation." I wonder, if He strips mercy from His creation other than the God-conscious, by what would the heavens, the earth, Paradise, Hell, those within them, the angels, and others remain and live, if no mercy encompasses them?

The better expression is what has been mentioned in some of our narrations—as I recall—that on that day, Allah shall gather the hundred for the believers. Gathering the hundred for them and applying it to them is different from withdrawing it from others and restricting it to them. The former is permissible and rational, while the latter is not. Understand this.

In the same source, al-Tabarani narrated from Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a tradition: "By Him in whose hand is my soul, Allah shall surely forgive on the Day of Resurrection with a forgiveness that Iblis will stretch himself out for, hoping that it might reach him."

**I say:** There is something of this meaning in the Shi'i chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Bakr al-Hudhali, who said: "When **وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ** '...My mercy embraces all things' (7:156) was revealed, Iblis said: 'O Lord, I am a thing.' Then **فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ** '...Soon I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious...' (7:156) was revealed, so Allah withdrew it from Iblis."

**I say:** It appears that this is a supposition and estimation by Abu Bakr. There is no doubt that Iblis benefits from the general mercy (الرحمة العامة) contained in the beginning of the verse, while being deprived of the specific mercy (الرحمة الخاصة) of the Hereafter contained in its conclusion.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from *Nahj al-Bayan*, it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: "Which of the creation is most amazing in faith?" They said: "The angels." He said: "The angels are with their Lord, so why should they not believe?" They said: "The prophets." He said: "The prophets receive revelation, so why should they not believe?" They said: "Us." He said: "I am among you, so why should you not believe? Rather, they are a people who will come after you; they will find a book in pages and believe in it." This is the meaning of His saying: **وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** "...and follow the light that has been sent down with him, it is they who are the successful" (7:157).

**I say:** There is no harm in this report; it is of the nature of "conceptual application" (الجري والانطباق) — *the application of a verse to instances beyond its original context*. In some narrations, the "light" (النور) is Ali (عليه السلام), which is also of the nature of conceptual application or the inner meaning (الباطن).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "The Children of Israel split after Moses into seventy-one sects, all of them in the Fire except one. The Christians split after Jesus into seventy-two sects, all of them in the Fire except one. And this nation shall split into seventy-three sects, all of them in the Fire except one. As for the Jews, Allah says: **وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** 'Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:159). As for the Christians, Allah says: **مِّنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ** '...among them is a moderate community...' (5:66); this is the one that is saved. As for us, we say: **وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** 'Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:181); this is the one that is saved from this nation."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu al-Sahban al-Bakri, who said: I heard Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) call the Exilarch (رأس الجالوت) and the Bishop of the Christians and say: "I am asking you about a matter that I know better than you, so do not hide it from me.

O Exilarch, by Him who sent down the Torah to Moses, fed them manna and quails, struck for them a dry path in the sea, and caused twelve springs to gush for them from the rock of the Mount—a spring for every tribe of the Children of Israel—inform me, into how many sects did the Children of Israel split after Moses?" He said: "One sect." Ali said: "You have lied, by Him besides whom there is no god! They split into seventy-one sects, all of them in the Fire except one, for Allah says: **وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** 'Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:159); this is the one that is saved."

In *al-Majma'*: "They are a people beyond China; between them and China is a valley of sand. They have not changed or altered." He said: "This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام)."

**I say:** The narration is weak and unacceptable. There is no news of this guiding and just Jewish nation today. If they existed today, they would be neither guiding nor guided, because the law (شريعة) of Moses was first abrogated by the law of Jesus (عليه السلام), and then both of their laws were abrogated by the law of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Therefore, some who cited this mythical story were forced to add that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) descended to them on the Night of Ascent (ليلة المعراج), called them, and they believed in him and he taught them the prayer.

They have fabricated strange and varied stories about them. It is narrated from Muqatil: "Among the things by which Allah favored Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is that on the Night of Ascent, he saw the people of Moses who are beyond China. This was because when the Children of Israel committed sins and killed those who commanded justice among the people, they supplicated their Lord while in the Holy Land, saying: 'O Allah, take us out from among them.' He answered them and made a tunnel for them in the earth, which they entered. He placed a flowing river with them and a lamp of light before them. They traveled through it for a year and a half, from Jerusalem to their current dwelling place. Allah brought them out to a land where insects, beasts, and predators mingle, yet there are no sins or acts of disobedience. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) came to them that night with Gabriel; they believed in him, confirmed him, and he taught them the prayer. They said that Moses had given them glad tidings of him."

It is narrated from al-Sha'bi, who said: "Indeed, Allah has servants beyond Andalusia—as far as the distance between us and Andalusia—who do not see that any creature has ever disobeyed Allah. Their pebbles are pearls and rubies, and their mountains are gold and silver. They do not sow, nor harvest, nor perform any labor. They have trees at their doors with broad leaves that serve as their clothing, and they have trees at their doors with fruit from which they eat."

There are other such accounts regarding their story, all of which are fabricated. You have already learned the meaning of the verse in the preceding commentary.
